FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study Year 2 (2016-2018 baseline, 2018-2020 follow-up); N=9,429 adolescents; explicitly states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis; multiple logistic regression with reported AOR estimates

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper; no evidence of duplication or preprint status

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper analyzes cyberbullying victimization and perpetration as outcomes. Screen time is measured but only as a covariate/predictor alongside sociodemographic factors (sex, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, income, education). Cyberbullying alone without a separately-measured smartphone or social media variable fails FT-IC2. The other side consists primarily of demographic and contextual predictors, not individual-trait-level variables paired with a qualifying SM/phone variable.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2 (cyberbullying-only outcome without separately-measured SM/phone variable as IV or DV)

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline (2016-2018); N=10,755; 9-10 year-old children; clear identification as "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis; multiple linear regression; sociodemographic associations with screen time modalities

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper in The Journal of Pediatrics; no preprint or duplication indicators

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper isolates smartphone/SM use per modality: "social networking, texting, video chat, video games, YouTube videos" are measured separately from TV/console. Methods section explicitly reports 6 contemporary screen time modalities individually. Sociodemographic factors (sex, race/ethnicity, income, parental education) on the other side are individual-trait-level demographic attributes. Per criteria §2.1, sociodemographic differences constitute individual-level variation.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients (B values) reported with 95% CI and p-values for all screen time modalities by sociodemographic factors in Table III and stratified analyses in Table IV

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: B coefficients, 95% CI, p-values for associations between screen time modalities and sociodemographic characteristics; linear regression estimates across television, videos, gaming, social networking, texting, video chat

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline data); FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: INCLUDE. Paper reports associations between screen time modalities and sociodemographic factors using linear regression with quantified results.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study December 2020 COVID-19 survey; N=4,106 adolescents ages 12-14; baseline 2016-2018; states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD)"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; modified Poisson regression with risk ratios; secondary analysis of December 2020 COVID-19 survey data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper in Preventive Medicine Reports; no preprint or duplication indicators

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures frequency of COVID-19 information exposure via social media, television media, internet, and parental counseling; masking behavior is the outcome (individual health behavior construct). SM/internet/TV information exposure is the IV; masking is the DV. Masking behavior is an individual-trait-level health protective behavior. Qualifies as SM/media paired with an individual-trait health outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression with adjusted risk ratios (ARR) reported; Table 2 shows ARR for COVID social media (1.09, 95% CI 1.00-1.19), television (1.09, 95% CI 1.01-1.17), parental counseling (1.28, 95% CI 1.20-1.36)

FT_IC4.evidence: Risk ratio estimates with 95% confidence intervals and p-values reported for all information sources; both unadjusted and adjusted models presented in Table 2

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (December 2020 COVID-19 survey); FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: INCLUDE. Paper reports associations between COVID-19 information sources (social media, television, internet, parental counseling) and masking behaviors with quantified risk ratios.
